questions.question: Which is the largest ocean in the world?
questions.explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean, covering more area than all the land on Earth combined!
questions.funFact: The Pacific Ocean is so big that it contains more than half of all the water on Earth!

questions.question: How many oceans are there on Earth?
questions.explanation: There are 5 oceans: Pacific, Atlantic, Indian, Southern (Antarctic), and Arctic.
questions.funFact: Together, all five oceans cover more than 70% of the Earth's surface. That means most of our planet is water!

questions.question: Which is the smallest ocean in the world?
questions.explanation: The Arctic Ocean is the smallest and shallowest of the five oceans. It is located around the North Pole.
questions.funFact: The Arctic Ocean is mostly covered with ice for much of the year, and polar bears live on that ice!

questions.question: Which ocean is named after a country?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean is named after India. It is the third-largest ocean in the world.
questions.funFact: The Indian Ocean is the warmest ocean in the world. Its warm waters are home to beautiful coral reefs!

questions.question: Which ocean touches the west coast of India?
questions.explanation: The Arabian Sea, which is part of the Indian Ocean, touches the west coast of India.
questions.funFact: The Arabian Sea gets its name because it connects India to the Arabian Peninsula, and traders have sailed across it for thousands of years!

questions.question: What is the largest animal in the ocean?
questions.explanation: The Blue Whale is the largest animal in the ocean and the largest animal that has ever lived on Earth!
questions.funFact: A Blue Whale's heart is so big that a small child could crawl through its arteries!

questions.question: What colour does the ocean usually look from above?
questions.explanation: The ocean usually looks blue because water absorbs red light and reflects blue light from the sun.
questions.funFact: In some places, the ocean can look green, turquoise, or even dark black depending on what is in the water and how deep it is!

questions.question: Is ocean water salty or sweet?
questions.explanation: Ocean water is salty! The salt comes from rocks on land that slowly dissolve and wash into the sea.
questions.funFact: If you took all the salt out of the ocean and spread it on land, it would make a layer more than 150 metres thick!

questions.question: Which of these animals lives in the ocean?
questions.explanation: Dolphins live in the ocean! They are very smart mammals that breathe air but spend their whole lives in water.
questions.funFact: Dolphins sleep with one eye open! Half their brain stays awake so they can keep breathing and watch for danger.

questions.question: Which ocean is at the bottom of the Earth, near Antarctica?
questions.explanation: The Southern Ocean (also called the Antarctic Ocean) surrounds Antarctica at the bottom of the Earth.
questions.funFact: The Southern Ocean is home to penguins, seals, and huge icebergs that can be as big as entire countries!

questions.question: What do we call a baby whale?
questions.explanation: A baby whale is called a calf, just like a baby cow! Whale mothers take great care of their calves.
questions.funFact: A baby Blue Whale drinks about 380 litres of its mother's milk every day and gains almost 90 kilograms each day!

questions.question: Which ocean is the second largest in the world?
questions.explanation: The Atlantic Ocean is the second-largest ocean. It separates the Americas from Europe and Africa.
questions.funFact: The Atlantic Ocean is getting wider by about 2.5 centimetres every year because the continents are slowly moving apart!

questions.question: Which sea is on the east coast of India?
questions.explanation: The Bay of Bengal is on the east coast of India. It is the largest bay in the world!
questions.funFact: The Bay of Bengal is home to the Sundarbans, the largest mangrove forest in the world, where Royal Bengal Tigers swim in salty water!

questions.question: How many arms does an octopus have?
questions.explanation: An octopus has 8 arms! Each arm has suckers on it that can taste and grip things.
questions.funFact: An octopus has three hearts and blue blood! Two hearts pump blood to the gills and one pumps it to the rest of the body.

questions.question: What is the deepest ocean in the world?
questions.explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the deepest ocean. Its deepest point, the Mariana Trench, goes down almost 11 kilometres!
questions.funFact: If you put Mount Everest at the bottom of the Mariana Trench, the top of the mountain would still be more than 2 kilometres underwater!

questions.question: What do sea turtles eat?
options: Jellyfish, seaweed, and small sea creatures
questions.explanation: Sea turtles eat different things depending on the species, including jellyfish, seaweed, and small sea creatures.
questions.funFact: Leatherback sea turtles love eating jellyfish! Sadly, they sometimes mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish, which is why we should not litter.

questions.question: What is a group of fish swimming together called?
questions.explanation: A group of fish swimming together is called a school. Fish swim in schools to stay safe from predators.
questions.funFact: Some schools of fish can have millions of fish in them! They move together so smoothly that they almost look like one giant creature.

questions.question: What are coral reefs made of?
options: Tiny living animals called coral polyps
questions.explanation: Coral reefs are made of tiny living animals called coral polyps. Over many years, their hard skeletons build up to form reefs.
questions.funFact: Coral reefs are called the rainforests of the sea because even though they cover less than 1% of the ocean floor, they are home to about 25% of all marine species!

questions.question: Which of these is NOT an ocean?
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean Sea is a sea, not an ocean! It is located between Europe, Africa, and Asia.
questions.funFact: The word Mediterranean means 'middle of the land' in Latin because the ancient Romans thought it was in the centre of the world!

questions.question: What do sharks use to breathe underwater?
questions.explanation: Sharks breathe using gills, which take in oxygen from the water as it flows over them.
questions.funFact: Some sharks have to keep swimming their whole lives to push water over their gills. If they stop, they cannot breathe!

questions.question: What is the name of the deepest part of the ocean?
questions.explanation: The Mariana Trench in the Pacific Ocean is the deepest known part of the ocean, reaching nearly 11,000 metres deep.
questions.funFact: The Mariana Trench is so deep that if you dropped a steel ball into it, the ball would take over an hour to reach the bottom!

questions.question: What causes ocean tides?
options: The gravitational pull of the Moon and Sun
questions.explanation: Ocean tides are mainly caused by the gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun on Earth's water.
questions.funFact: The Bay of Fundy in Canada has the highest tides in the world. The water level can change by up to 16 metres between high and low tide!

questions.question: What is the Great Barrier Reef?
options: A mountain range under the ocean
The world's largest coral reef system
questions.explanation: The Great Barrier Reef off the coast of Australia is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching over 2,300 kilometres.
questions.funFact: The Great Barrier Reef is so large that it can be seen from outer space! It is home to over 1,500 species of fish.

questions.question: What is the Gulf Stream?
options: A river in the ocean floor
A warm ocean current in the Atlantic Ocean
questions.explanation: The Gulf Stream is a powerful warm ocean current that flows from the Gulf of Mexico across the Atlantic Ocean toward Europe.
questions.funFact: The Gulf Stream carries so much warm water that it helps keep countries like the United Kingdom and Ireland much warmer than they would normally be!

questions.question: Which zone of the ocean gets the most sunlight?
questions.explanation: The Sunlight Zone (also called the Epipelagic Zone) is the top layer of the ocean, reaching down to about 200 metres. It gets the most sunlight.
questions.funFact: Even though the Sunlight Zone is only a small fraction of the ocean's depth, most ocean life lives here because sunlight allows plants and algae to grow!

questions.question: What are underwater mountains that rise above the ocean surface called?
questions.explanation: When underwater mountains (seamounts) rise above the ocean surface, they become islands. Many islands, like Hawaii, were formed this way by volcanic activity.
questions.funFact: Hawaii's Mauna Kea is actually taller than Mount Everest if measured from its base on the ocean floor. It stands over 10,000 metres from base to peak!

questions.question: What is a tsunami?
options: A very strong wind over the ocean
A giant wave caused by an underwater earthquake or volcanic eruption
questions.explanation: A tsunami is a series of giant waves usually caused by earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides under the ocean.
questions.funFact: In the deep ocean, a tsunami can travel as fast as a jet plane, at over 700 kilometres per hour, but it may only be a few centimetres tall!

questions.question: Which ocean animal can change its colour to blend in with its surroundings?
questions.explanation: Octopuses can quickly change their colour, pattern, and even texture to blend in with their surroundings for camouflage.
questions.funFact: An octopus can change colour in just 0.3 seconds! They use special cells called chromatophores in their skin to create incredible disguises.

questions.question: What is the Mid-Atlantic Ridge?
options: A coral reef in the Atlantic
An underwater mountain range running through the Atlantic Ocean
A deep trench in the Atlantic
A chain of islands in the Atlantic
questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is a massive underwater mountain range that runs down the middle of the Atlantic Ocean. It was formed where tectonic plates are pulling apart.
questions.funFact: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is the longest mountain range on Earth, stretching over 16,000 kilometres. Most of it is hidden deep underwater!

questions.question: What percentage of the Earth's surface is covered by oceans?
questions.explanation: About 71% of Earth's surface is covered by oceans. That is why Earth is sometimes called the Blue Planet!
questions.funFact: We have explored less than 20% of the ocean floor. We actually know more about the surface of the Moon than about the bottom of our own oceans!

questions.question: Which famous sea is so salty that people can easily float in it?
questions.explanation: The Dead Sea, located between Israel and Jordan, is so salty that people float on its surface without even trying.
questions.funFact: The Dead Sea is almost 10 times saltier than regular ocean water! It is called 'dead' because almost no fish or plants can live in such salty water.

questions.question: What is bioluminescence in the ocean?
options: The ability of some sea creatures to produce their own light
questions.explanation: Bioluminescence is the ability of certain marine organisms to produce their own light through chemical reactions in their bodies.
questions.funFact: About 80% of creatures living in the deep ocean can produce their own light! Some use it to attract prey, find mates, or confuse predators.

questions.question: What type of whale is known for its long, beautiful songs?
questions.explanation: Humpback Whales are famous for their long, complex songs that can last for hours. Only male humpbacks sing these songs.
questions.funFact: A Humpback Whale's song can travel thousands of kilometres through the ocean! All the males in one area sing the same song, and the song changes every year.

questions.question: What is the Ring of Fire?
options: An area of frequent earthquakes and volcanic eruptions around the Pacific Ocean
questions.explanation: The Ring of Fire is a horseshoe-shaped area around the Pacific Ocean where many earthquakes and volcanic eruptions happen.
questions.funFact: About 75% of the world's active volcanoes are located along the Ring of Fire, and about 90% of the world's earthquakes happen there!

questions.question: What is the Great Pacific Garbage Patch?
options: A recycling centre in the Pacific
A large area of floating plastic and trash in the Pacific Ocean
A patch of seaweed in the Pacific
questions.explanation: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is a huge collection of floating plastic and marine debris in the North Pacific Ocean, gathered together by ocean currents.
questions.funFact: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is estimated to be twice the size of Texas! Most of the debris is tiny pieces of microplastic that are hard to see.

questions.question: Which of the following animals is a mammal, not a fish?
questions.explanation: Seals are marine mammals. They breathe air, are warm-blooded, and feed their babies milk, just like other mammals.
questions.funFact: Seals can hold their breath for up to 2 hours while diving! Elephant Seals can dive more than 1,500 metres deep to find food.

questions.question: What is plankton?
options: Tiny organisms that drift in the ocean and form the base of the food chain
Small rocks on the ocean floor
questions.explanation: Plankton are tiny organisms, both plant-like (phytoplankton) and animal-like (zooplankton), that drift in the ocean. They form the base of the ocean food chain.
questions.funFact: Phytoplankton produce about 50% of all the oxygen on Earth through photosynthesis. Every other breath you take comes from these tiny ocean organisms!

questions.question: Which Indian island group is known for its beautiful coral reefs?
questions.explanation: Both the Andaman and Nicobar Islands and the Lakshadweep Islands have beautiful coral reefs. Lakshadweep is actually a group of coral atolls!
questions.funFact: Lakshadweep means 'a hundred thousand islands' in Sanskrit. These islands are actually the tops of ancient coral reefs that grew up from the ocean floor!

questions.question: What is ocean acidification?
options: The ocean becoming fresher due to ice melting
The ocean absorbing excess carbon dioxide, making it more acidic
Pollution from factories flowing into the ocean
Volcanic eruptions releasing acid into the sea
questions.explanation: Ocean acidification occurs when the ocean absorbs excess carbon dioxide (CO2) from the atmosphere, which reacts with seawater to form carbonic acid, lowering the ocean's pH.
questions.funFact: Since the Industrial Revolution, the ocean has become about 30% more acidic. This makes it harder for shellfish, corals, and some plankton to build their shells and skeletons!

questions.question: Who was the first person to reach the bottom of the Mariana Trench?
questions.explanation: In 1960, Jacques Piccard and Don Walsh descended to the bottom of the Mariana Trench in the bathyscaphe Trieste, reaching a depth of about 10,916 metres.
questions.funFact: When Piccard and Walsh reached the bottom, they were amazed to see a flatfish swimming. This proved that life exists even at the most extreme depths of the ocean!

questions.question: What is a hydrothermal vent?
options: A hole in the ocean floor that releases cold fresh water
A crack in the ocean floor that releases superheated mineral-rich water
An underwater volcano that erupts regularly
questions.explanation: Hydrothermal vents are cracks in the ocean floor where seawater seeps down, gets superheated by magma, and shoots back up loaded with minerals.
questions.funFact: Water coming out of hydrothermal vents can be over 400 degrees Celsius! Unique ecosystems thrive there, powered by chemicals instead of sunlight.

questions.question: What is thermohaline circulation?
options: A global system of deep ocean currents driven by differences in water temperature and salinity
The circular pattern of surface waves
questions.explanation: Thermohaline circulation is a global system of deep ocean currents driven by differences in water density, which is affected by temperature (thermo) and salt content (haline).
questions.funFact: Thermohaline circulation is sometimes called the global ocean conveyor belt. It takes about 1,000 years for water to complete one full loop around the world!

questions.question: What causes a spring tide?
options: Strong winds during the spring season
The Sun, Moon, and Earth lining up during full and new moons
Warm water from spring rivers entering the ocean
questions.explanation: Spring tides occur when the Sun, Moon, and Earth are aligned (during full and new moons), combining their gravitational pull to create extra high and extra low tides.
questions.funFact: Spring tides have nothing to do with the spring season! The name comes from the German word 'springen' meaning to leap, because the water seems to leap up higher.

questions.question: What is the abyssal zone of the ocean?
options: The surface layer warmed by the sun
The deep ocean zone between about 4,000-6,000 metres deep
questions.explanation: The abyssal zone lies between roughly 4,000 and 6,000 metres deep. It is completely dark, near freezing, and under immense pressure.
questions.funFact: The abyssal zone covers about 60% of Earth's surface, making it the largest habitat on the planet. Yet it remains one of the least explored places on Earth!

questions.question: What is El Nino?
options: A type of tropical fish found in the Pacific
A periodic warming of sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean
A permanent warm current near Australia
A hurricane that forms near the equator
questions.explanation: El Nino is a climate pattern where sea surface temperatures in the central and eastern Pacific Ocean become unusually warm, affecting weather patterns worldwide.
questions.funFact: El Nino means 'The Little Boy' or 'Christ Child' in Spanish because South American fishermen noticed the warm waters often appeared around Christmas time!

questions.question: What is the continental shelf?
options: The deepest part of the ocean floor
The gently sloping underwater edge of a continent
A flat area in the middle of the ocean
The ridge between two tectonic plates
questions.explanation: The continental shelf is the gently sloping underwater edge of a continent that extends from the shoreline to where the ocean floor drops steeply into the deep ocean.
questions.funFact: Even though continental shelves make up only about 8% of the ocean floor, they are incredibly important. About 90% of all fish caught commercially come from waters above the continental shelf!

questions.question: What role do mangrove forests play in the ocean ecosystem?
options: They produce most of the ocean's oxygen
They act as nurseries for marine life and protect coastlines from erosion and storms
They filter salt out of ocean water
questions.explanation: Mangrove forests grow along tropical coastlines and serve as nurseries for young fish and other marine life. Their roots also protect shorelines from erosion, waves, and storm surges.
questions.funFact: India's Sundarbans is the largest mangrove forest in the world, covering about 10,000 square kilometres across India and Bangladesh. It is home to the famous Bengal Tiger!

questions.question: Who is known as the father of modern oceanography?
questions.explanation: Matthew Fontaine Maury, a 19th-century American naval officer, is often called the father of modern oceanography. He created detailed maps of ocean winds and currents.
questions.funFact: Maury's work mapping ocean currents saved ships many days of travel time. His book 'The Physical Geography of the Sea' published in 1855 was the first major textbook on oceanography!

questions.question: What is coral bleaching?
options: Coral turning white because humans paint it
Coral expelling the algae living in it due to stress from warm water, causing it to turn white
Coral naturally changing colour with the seasons
questions.explanation: Coral bleaching happens when stressed corals (usually from warm water) expel the colourful algae (zooxanthellae) living in their tissues, turning the coral white.
questions.funFact: The algae that live inside coral provide up to 90% of the coral's energy through photosynthesis. Without them, the coral can starve and die if conditions do not improve!

questions.question: What is the Sargasso Sea unique for?
options: It is the saltiest sea in the world
It is the only sea with no land boundaries, defined by ocean currents
It is the smallest sea on Earth
questions.explanation: The Sargasso Sea in the North Atlantic is the only sea in the world that has no land boundaries. It is defined by four ocean currents that circulate around it.
questions.funFact: The Sargasso Sea is named after Sargassum, a type of floating seaweed that covers its surface. European eels travel thousands of kilometres to lay their eggs there!

questions.question: What is the hard, protective home that a hermit crab carries on its back?
questions.explanation: Hermit crabs do not grow their own shells. Instead, they find empty shells from other animals, like snails, and carry them on their backs for protection.
questions.funFact: When a hermit crab outgrows its shell, it has to find a bigger one! Sometimes hermit crabs line up by size and swap shells in a chain so everyone gets an upgrade.

questions.question: What do we call the place where the ocean meets the land?
questions.explanation: The coast is the place where the ocean meets the land. Beaches, cliffs, and rocky shores are all types of coastline.
questions.funFact: If you added up all the coastlines in the world, they would stretch over 1.6 million kilometres. That is enough to go around the Earth more than 40 times!

questions.question: How do dolphins talk to each other?
questions.explanation: Dolphins communicate with each other using a variety of clicks, whistles, and squeaks. Each dolphin even has its own special whistle, like a name!
questions.funFact: Scientists have discovered that every dolphin develops its own unique signature whistle as a baby. Other dolphins use that whistle to call them, just like calling someone by name!

questions.question: What is the biggest type of shark?
questions.explanation: The Whale Shark is the biggest shark and the biggest fish in the world. It can grow up to 12 metres long or even more!
questions.funFact: Even though Whale Sharks are enormous, they are very gentle and eat only tiny plankton and small fish. They are sometimes called gentle giants of the sea!

questions.question: What ocean animal has a body shaped like a star?
questions.explanation: A starfish, also called a sea star, has a body shaped like a star. Most starfish have five arms, but some can have up to 40!
questions.funFact: If a starfish loses one of its arms, it can grow a brand new one! Some starfish can even regrow their entire body from just one arm.

questions.question: What do whales spray out of the hole on top of their heads?
options: Warm air and a little bit of water
questions.explanation: Whales breathe through a blowhole on top of their heads. When they come to the surface, they blow out warm air that mixes with cool air and looks like a big spray or mist.
questions.funFact: A Blue Whale's blowhole spray can shoot up to 9 metres in the air. That is almost as tall as a three-storey building!

questions.question: What are the tiny, colourful fish that live inside sea anemones?
questions.explanation: Clownfish live inside sea anemones. The anemone's stinging tentacles protect the clownfish, and in return, the clownfish keeps the anemone clean.
questions.funFact: Clownfish have a special slimy coating on their bodies that protects them from the anemone's sting. Without this coating, the anemone would sting them just like any other fish!

questions.question: Where do penguins live in the wild?
options: In the Southern Hemisphere, mostly near Antarctica
questions.explanation: Penguins live in the Southern Hemisphere, with many species found near Antarctica. They are excellent swimmers even though they cannot fly!
questions.funFact: Emperor Penguins can hold their breath for over 20 minutes and dive more than 500 metres deep to catch fish. That is deeper than most submarines go!

questions.question: What is the Twilight Zone of the ocean?
options: The zone right at the surface
The zone between 200 and 1,000 metres deep where very little light reaches
The zone at the very bottom of the ocean
The zone where ocean meets freshwater
questions.explanation: The Twilight Zone, also called the Mesopelagic Zone, lies between 200 and 1,000 metres deep. Only a tiny amount of sunlight reaches this zone, creating a dim, twilight-like environment.
questions.funFact: Scientists estimate there may be more fish in the Twilight Zone than in all the rest of the ocean combined. Many of these fish migrate to the surface at night to feed!

questions.question: What is a kelp forest?
options: A forest of trees growing in shallow water
A dense underwater area of tall seaweed called kelp
questions.explanation: Kelp forests are dense underwater areas of tall brown seaweed called kelp. They grow in cool, shallow waters and provide shelter and food for many marine animals.
questions.funFact: Giant kelp can grow up to 60 centimetres in a single day, making it one of the fastest growing organisms on Earth! Some kelp forests can be as tall as a 15-storey building.

questions.question: Which strait separates Asia from North America?
questions.explanation: The Bering Strait separates Asia (Russia) from North America (Alaska). It is only about 85 kilometres wide at its narrowest point.
questions.funFact: During the last Ice Age, the Bering Strait was dry land because sea levels were much lower. Ancient humans are believed to have walked across this land bridge from Asia to North America!

questions.question: What causes waves in the ocean?
options: Wind blowing across the surface of the water
questions.explanation: Most ocean waves are caused by wind blowing across the surface of the water. The stronger the wind blows and the longer it blows, the bigger the waves become.
questions.funFact: The tallest wave ever recorded by a buoy was about 19 metres high in the North Atlantic Ocean in 2013. That is taller than a six-storey building!

questions.question: How long can a sea turtle hold its breath underwater?
questions.explanation: Sea turtles can hold their breath for up to 5 hours while resting or sleeping underwater, though they usually come up more often when they are active.
questions.funFact: Sea turtles have been around for over 100 million years, which means they lived alongside the dinosaurs! They are one of the oldest groups of reptiles on Earth.

questions.question: What is an ocean current?
options: A wave that crashes on the shore
A continuous flow of water in a certain direction through the ocean
A whirlpool at the bottom of the sea
The tide going in and out
questions.explanation: An ocean current is a continuous, directed flow of ocean water. Currents are caused by wind, differences in water temperature and salinity, and the rotation of the Earth.
questions.funFact: Ocean currents move huge amounts of water around the planet. The Antarctic Circumpolar Current carries 100 times more water than all the rivers in the world combined!

questions.question: What are microplastics?
options: Very tiny pieces of plastic less than 5 millimetres in size
Plastic toys shaped like sea creatures
Plastic that dissolves in salt water
questions.explanation: Microplastics are tiny pieces of plastic less than 5 millimetres in size. They come from larger plastic items breaking down or from products like cosmetics and synthetic clothing.
questions.funFact: Scientists have found microplastics everywhere in the ocean, from the surface to the bottom of the Mariana Trench. They have even been found inside deep-sea creatures and Arctic ice!

questions.question: What is an atoll?
options: A ring-shaped coral reef surrounding a lagoon
questions.explanation: An atoll is a ring-shaped coral reef, island, or chain of islands that surrounds a shallow lagoon. Atolls form when coral grows around a volcanic island that later sinks below the surface.
questions.funFact: The Maldives in the Indian Ocean is made up of 26 atolls with over 1,000 coral islands. The entire country is less than 2.4 metres above sea level, making it the flattest country on Earth!

questions.question: What are chemosynthetic organisms?
options: Organisms that get energy from sunlight
Organisms that get energy from chemical reactions rather than sunlight
Organisms that eat other deep-sea creatures
questions.explanation: Chemosynthetic organisms produce energy through chemical reactions, often using hydrogen sulphide or methane, instead of relying on sunlight like plants do through photosynthesis.
questions.funFact: Entire ecosystems around hydrothermal vents are powered by chemosynthesis. Giant tube worms living near these vents can grow up to 2 metres long and have no mouth or stomach. They rely entirely on chemosynthetic bacteria living inside them!

questions.question: What is the scientific term for the salt content of ocean water?
questions.explanation: Salinity is the scientific term for the concentration of dissolved salts in water. The average salinity of ocean water is about 35 parts per thousand, meaning every kilogram of seawater contains about 35 grams of salt.
questions.funFact: The saltiest body of water on Earth is not the Dead Sea but actually Don Juan Pond in Antarctica, which has a salinity of over 400 parts per thousand. It is so salty that it never freezes, even at minus 50 degrees Celsius!

questions.question: What is a marine protected area (MPA)?
options: An aquarium that rescues injured sea animals
A section of the ocean where human activity is restricted to protect ecosystems
A beach that is closed to tourists
A harbour where ships are repaired
questions.explanation: A Marine Protected Area is a clearly defined section of the ocean where human activities such as fishing, drilling, and development are regulated or restricted to conserve marine ecosystems and biodiversity.
questions.funFact: As of recent years, only about 8% of the world's oceans are designated as marine protected areas. Scientists recommend protecting at least 30% of the ocean by 2030 to maintain healthy marine ecosystems!

questions.question: How do tectonic plates create mid-ocean ridges?
options: Two plates collide and push upward
Two plates slide past each other horizontally
Two plates pull apart and magma rises to fill the gap
A single plate folds over on itself
questions.explanation: Mid-ocean ridges form at divergent plate boundaries where two tectonic plates pull apart. As they separate, magma rises from beneath the Earth's crust to fill the gap, creating new ocean floor.
questions.funFact: The process of new ocean floor being created at mid-ocean ridges is called seafloor spreading. It was first proposed by Harry Hess in the 1960s and helped prove the theory of plate tectonics!

questions.question: What is the role of the ocean in regulating Earth's climate?
options: The ocean has very little effect on climate
The ocean absorbs and distributes heat and absorbs carbon dioxide, regulating global temperatures
The ocean only affects the climate of coastal areas
The ocean makes the climate warmer everywhere
questions.explanation: The ocean plays a crucial role in regulating Earth's climate by absorbing about 90% of excess heat and roughly 25% of carbon dioxide produced by human activities. Ocean currents also distribute heat around the planet.
questions.funFact: The top 3 metres of the ocean hold as much heat energy as the entire atmosphere! Without the ocean absorbing excess heat, average global temperatures would be much higher than they are today.

questions.question: What is an anoxic dead zone in the ocean?
options: An area with no marine life due to extreme cold
An area with very low oxygen levels where most marine life cannot survive
A region with extremely high salinity
The deepest part of an ocean trench
questions.explanation: Anoxic dead zones are areas in the ocean with very low dissolved oxygen levels (hypoxia). They are often caused by excess nutrients from agricultural runoff that trigger algal blooms, which consume oxygen as they decompose.
questions.funFact: One of the largest dead zones in the world forms every summer in the Gulf of Mexico, caused by fertiliser runoff from farms along the Mississippi River. It can grow to the size of the state of New Jersey!

questions.question: What is the Coriolis effect and how does it influence ocean currents?
options: It is the warming of water near the equator that drives currents
It is the deflection of moving objects caused by Earth's rotation, causing currents to curve
It is the gravitational pull of the Moon on ocean water
It is the effect of wind speed on wave height
questions.explanation: The Coriolis effect is caused by Earth's rotation. It deflects moving objects, including ocean currents, to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere, creating large circular current patterns called gyres.
questions.funFact: The Coriolis effect is the reason that hurricanes spin counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere and clockwise in the Southern Hemisphere. Right on the equator, the Coriolis effect is zero!

questions.question: What are submarine canyons?
options: Deep, V-shaped valleys cut into the continental shelf and slope
Flat plains on the deep ocean floor
questions.explanation: Submarine canyons are deep, steep-walled valleys cut into the continental shelf and slope. They are often formed by turbidity currents, which are underwater landslides of sediment, or by ancient rivers when sea levels were lower.
questions.funFact: The Monterey Canyon off the coast of California is as deep and large as the Grand Canyon! It plunges nearly 2,000 metres below the ocean surface and is home to a rich variety of marine life.

questions.question: How does sea level rise from climate change threaten coastal ecosystems?
options: It only affects human buildings, not natural ecosystems
It can flood wetlands, erode beaches, increase saltwater intrusion, and destroy habitats
It makes the ocean cleaner and healthier
It only raises sea levels by a few millimetres so it has no real effect
questions.explanation: Sea level rise caused by melting ice sheets and thermal expansion of warming water threatens coastal ecosystems by flooding wetlands and marshes, eroding beaches, increasing saltwater intrusion into freshwater habitats, and destroying nesting areas for sea turtles and shorebirds.
questions.funFact: Global sea levels have risen about 20 centimetres since 1900 and the rate is accelerating. Some low-lying island nations like Tuvalu and the Marshall Islands may become uninhabitable within this century if the trend continues!
